name: Apple III
desc: Apple's business computer, plagued from launch by overheating. Engineers removed the fan to make it silent, so chips warped and fell off the board.

name: Apple Lisa
desc: Apple's first GUI-based personal computer at $9,995. Technically remarkable, commercially ruinous. Outclassed and outpriced by the Mac a year later.

name: Apple QuickTake
desc: One of the first consumer digital cameras, made by Fujifilm and sold under Apple's name. Steve Jobs killed the entire product line within weeks of returning to Apple.

name: Pippin
desc: Apple's multimedia gaming platform co-developed with Bandai. It sold just 42,000 units and cost nearly $600 at launch. An ambitious experiment in all the wrong directions.

name: Newton MessagePad
desc: Apple's pioneering PDA, famous for ambitious handwriting recognition that became a punchline. Steve Jobs canceled it two weeks after taking back the CEO seat.

name: Power Mac G4 Cube
desc: A fanless, silent 8-inch cube of a Mac and a genuine design triumph. But at $1,799 for a machine slower than the Power Mac G4 tower, it was a commercial failure.

name: iTools
desc: Apple's first internet services bundle, offering a free @mac.com email address and iDisk cloud storage. Replaced by the paid .Mac subscription service.

name: HyperCard
desc: A revolutionary hypermedia and scripting environment that predated and inspired the World Wide Web. Quietly abandoned, then left to wither for a decade before being killed entirely.

name: iPod mini
desc: The colorful, compact iPod that made music fashionable. It became Apple's best-selling product, then was discontinued to make way for the even thinner iPod nano.

name: Shake
desc: Professional VFX compositing software used in countless Hollywood films. Apple acquired it, charged for it, then killed it in 2009 without offering any replacement.

name: Apple Cinema Display
desc: Apple's aluminum widescreen monitors, beloved by designers and video editors. Quietly discontinued in 2011, leaving professionals without an Apple display option.

name: Final Cut Server
desc: A collaborative media asset management system for Final Cut Pro workflows in studios and broadcast. Discontinued alongside the controversial Final Cut Pro X launch.

name: Front Row
desc: A full-screen media center app for Macs, designed to be controlled from the couch with the Apple Remote. Killed in OS X Lion without a replacement.

name: iDVD
desc: DVD menu and disc authoring software bundled with every Mac. Made beautiful animated menus, until optical drives quietly disappeared from Apple hardware forever.

name: iWeb
desc: Drag-and-drop personal website creator, tightly integrated with MobileMe hosting. When MobileMe shut down, iWeb had nowhere to publish, so it died too.

name: Logic Express
desc: The affordable entry point to professional music production on Mac. Killed when Apple slashed Logic Pro 9's price to $199, eliminating the need for a budget tier.

name: Rosetta
desc: The translation layer that let PowerPC apps run on Intel Macs, enabling Apple's first major chip transition without breaking software. Removed in OS X Lion.

name: Xserve
desc: Apple's rack-mounted 1U server hardware and one of Apple's few genuine enterprise products. Quietly discontinued in favor of telling enterprises to buy a Mac Pro.

name: iTunes Ping
desc: Apple's music-focused social network. Steve Jobs personally called Mark Zuckerberg to negotiate a Facebook tie-in. Zuckerberg asked for too much; Ping launched anyway and flopped.

name: MobileMe
desc: Apple's cloud sync and email service. So unreliable at launch that Steve Jobs called it an embarrassment to the entire company at an internal meeting. Replaced by iCloud.

name: Aperture
desc: Apple's professional photo management and RAW editing suite, and Lightroom's closest rival for years. Killed and replaced by Photos, which couldn't do a fraction of what it did.

name: iPod classic
desc: The original click-wheel iPod that started everything. Discontinued after 13 years not by choice, but because a key internal component was no longer manufactured anywhere.

name: Beats Music
desc: Dr. Dre's streaming service, praised for human-curated playlists. Apple acquired Beats for $3 billion and shut down Beats Music within months to build Apple Music.

name: iPhoto
desc: The photo management app bundled with every Mac for over 13 years. Replaced by Photos, a simpler, more iCloud-centric app that lost many of iPhoto's organizing features.

name: iAd
desc: Apple's mobile advertising platform. Steve Jobs claimed it would "reinvent" advertising. It never seriously challenged Google. Shut down after six increasingly quiet years.

name: iPhone Headphone Jack
desc: The 3.5mm audio port was present on every iPhone for 9 years. Apple called its removal "courageous" in 2016 and included a Lightning adapter in the box. The box no longer ships.

name: QuickTime for Windows
desc: Apple's media player for Windows, kept alive for over 20 years. Killed in 2016 with critical security vulnerabilities still unpatched. Users were advised to uninstall immediately.

name: Thunderbolt Display
desc: Apple's last standalone external monitor before the Pro Display XDR. Discontinued in 2016 without a replacement, leaving professional Mac users without an Apple display for three full years.

name: 32-bit iOS App Support
desc: iOS 11 dropped support for 32-bit apps, rendering thousands of games and apps permanently unplayable. Many developers had already moved on; many players hadn't.

name: iPod nano
desc: The ultra-thin, colorful iPod that evolved through seven generations and countless designs. A design icon of the 2000s and 2010s, killed alongside its sibling with no ceremony.

name: iPod shuffle
desc: The tiny clip-on music player with no screen, just shuffle. Simple, cheap, and genuinely fun. The only music player you could wear like a badge.

name: AirPort Express
desc: Apple's compact Wi-Fi router that doubled as a wired AirPlay audio receiver, beloved by audiophiles who wanted wireless hi-fi without the hassle. Gone with the rest of the AirPort line.

name: AirPort Extreme
desc: Apple's flagship home Wi-Fi router with its striking cylindrical tower design and seamless Time Machine integration. Discontinued as Apple exited the router business entirely.

name: Time Capsule
desc: A Wi-Fi router with a built-in hard drive for automatic, wireless Time Machine backups. An elegant hardware solution to an everyday problem, killed alongside the AirPort line.

name: iTunes
desc: The app that defined the digital music era, powered the iPod revolution, and built the App Store. After 18 years of bloat, replaced by Music, Podcasts, and TV apps in macOS Catalina.

name: MacBook (12-inch)
desc: Apple's thinnest, lightest laptop, with a single USB-C port, fanless design, and a beautiful Retina display. Too compromised for most buyers; too underpowered to justify its price.

name: 32-bit macOS App Support
desc: macOS Catalina dropped 32-bit app support entirely, breaking thousands of games and legacy applications with no migration path and no warning beyond a single OS version.

name: Texture
desc: A magazine subscription app dubbed "the Netflix of magazines." Apple acquired it in 2018 and quietly folded it into Apple News+, without keeping all of its content agreements.

name: Apple Remote
desc: The minimalist aluminum infrared remote, designed for controlling Macs and Apple TV from across the room. Quietly discontinued in 2021 as the Siri Remote took its place.

name: iTunes U
desc: Platform for universities, schools, and educators to share lectures and course materials through iTunes. Quietly shut down and partially absorbed into Apple Podcasts.

name: iPod touch
desc: The last surviving iPod, an iPhone without the cellular contract. Beloved by kids, travelers, and budget-conscious music fans for 15 years. The end of an era.

name: macOS Server
desc: Apple's server OS, once a serious enterprise product with directory services, file sharing, and device management. Wound down over years until it became just a few GUI wrappers.

name: HomePod (1st gen)
desc: Apple's premium smart speaker with genuinely excellent audio quality. Discontinued to consolidate around the HomePod mini. Sales never justified its $349 price tag.

name: iPhone SE (3rd gen)
desc: Apple's last affordable iPhone, and the last iPhone with a Home button and Touch ID. Quietly discontinued as Apple consolidated its lineup around Face ID.

name: Macintosh TV
desc: A Mac LC 520 in a black case with a built-in TV tuner, and Apple's first attempt at merging computing and television. Only 10,000 units were ever made and it was never sold outside the US.

name: eMate 300
desc: A Newton-powered clamshell laptop built for students, with an integrated keyboard and weeks of battery life. Killed alongside the Newton when Steve Jobs returned.

name: AppleWorks
desc: Apple's bundled office suite, born as ClarisWorks in 1991. For 16 years it was the word processor and spreadsheet for millions of Mac and Apple II users. Retired when iWork arrived.

name: .Mac
desc: The paid successor to iTools: @mac.com email, iDisk cloud storage, and online sync for $99/year. Frequently unreliable. Rebranded as the even more unreliable MobileMe.

name: iSync
desc: A Mac utility for syncing contacts, calendars, and bookmarks across Macs, phones, and iPods over Bluetooth or USB. Essential before iCloud made the whole idea obsolete.

name: eMac
desc: An education-focused all-in-one Mac built around a 17-inch CRT. Initially sold only to schools, briefly offered to consumers. The last Mac ever shipped with a cathode-ray tube.

name: iSight
desc: Apple's sleek external FireWire webcam: aluminum, cylindrical, and perfectly designed. Made obsolete when Apple built cameras into every Mac and display it sold.

name: 30-pin Dock Connector
desc: The wide 30-pin connector used on every iPod and iPhone for nine years. It spawned an entire accessories industry: alarm clocks, car adapters, speaker docks. Killed overnight by Lightning.

name: WebObjects
desc: A powerful web application server born at NeXT that Apple used to run the iTunes Store. Quietly deprecated in 2016 with no migration path for the enterprise customers who relied on it.

name: Game Center
desc: Apple's social gaming network with achievements, leaderboards, and multiplayer matchmaking. The standalone app was silently removed in iOS 10, Apple's quiet admission that no one used it.

name: Apple TV (3rd gen)
desc: The streaming set-top box that never got an App Store. It played video and little else. Quietly discontinued once the 4th-gen Apple TV finally delivered the platform Jobs had promised for years.

name: iTunes Radio
desc: Apple's free ad-supported streaming radio service, built into iTunes and iOS. A direct Pandora rival, folded into Apple Music within three years without so much as a goodbye.

name: Newsstand
desc: A dedicated iOS app folder for digital magazine and newspaper subscriptions. Publishers barely supported it; readers rarely opened it. Removed in iOS 9 and absorbed into Apple News.

name: 3D Touch
desc: Pressure-sensitive display tech enabling Peek, Pop, and shortcut menus based on press force. Quietly dropped from the iPhone XR in 2018, abandoned entirely with the iPhone 11.

name: Mac Pro (Trash Can)
desc: The radical cylindrical Mac Pro: striking to look at, thermally compromised inside. Apple went six years without an update and publicly admitted they had "painted themselves into a corner."

name: Remote (iOS app)
desc: Apple's official app for controlling iTunes and Apple TV from an iPhone. Downloaded by millions and used daily for over a decade. Silently pulled from the App Store in 2021.

name: Dashboard
desc: macOS's dedicated layer of mini-apps called widgets, accessed with a keystroke. Groundbreaking in 2004, a memory hog by 2014, and finally removed in macOS Monterey after 17 years of neglect.

name: Network Utility
desc: A Mac app for network diagnostics: ping, traceroute, port scanning, Whois lookups. A staple for sysadmins and power users for over 20 years. Removed from macOS Monterey without any warning.

name: Touch Bar
desc: The OLED strip that replaced the function key row on MacBook Pros. A programmable, context-sensitive control surface that most users ignored, most developers never supported, and Apple quietly dropped.

name: Lightning Connector
desc: Apple's slim, reversible 8-pin connector, used on every iPhone for over a decade. Replaced by USB-C in 2023 after EU regulations made proprietary connectors legally untenable.

name: AirPower
desc: Apple's wireless charging mat, announced in 2017 to charge iPhone, AirPods, and Apple Watch all at once on any spot. Cancelled in 2019 after Apple admitted it could not meet its own quality standards. The first product Apple ever announced and never shipped.

name: iMac G3
desc: The translucent Bondi Blue all-in-one that saved Apple from bankruptcy. Designed by Jony Ive, eventually offered in five fruity colors, and sold over six million units. The product that proved Apple could still ship something the world wanted.

name: iMac G4
desc: The sunflower iMac, with a flat-panel display floating on a chrome arm above a hemispherical dome. Beloved by designers and discontinued after just 30 months when Apple decided the screen should be the computer.

name: iBook
desc: The colorful consumer laptop, born as the G3 clamshell in tangerine and blueberry, reborn in 2003 as a refined white slab. Retired when Apple switched to Intel and replaced it with the MacBook.

name: Power Mac G5
desc: Apple's aluminum tower with a perforated cheese-grater face, promoted as the world's fastest personal computer. Steve Jobs promised 3GHz within a year. It never came, and Apple jumped to Intel two years later.

name: PowerBook
desc: Apple's professional laptop line for 15 years, evolving from the chunky 100 series to the titanium and aluminum G4. Retired when Apple switched to Intel and rebranded the line as MacBook Pro.

name: Hockey Puck Mouse
desc: The round USB mouse that shipped with the original iMac G3. Impossible to orient by feel and hated by every reviewer who touched it. Replaced within two years by the Apple Pro Mouse and remembered as a cautionary tale in industrial design.

name: Mighty Mouse
desc: Apple's first multi-button mouse, with a tiny clickable scroll ball on top. The ball clogged with skin oils within months, leaving users frantically rolling it upside down on paper to bring it back to life.

name: iPod Hi-Fi
desc: Apple's $349 boombox speaker dock for the iPod, personally championed by Steve Jobs at its launch as audiophile-quality. Discontinued 18 months later as the rest of the speaker industry caught up and sold the same thing for less.

name: Smart Battery Case
desc: Apple's official iPhone case with a built-in battery, instantly recognizable for the hunchback bulge that broke every other iPhone case design rule. Quietly discontinued in 2020 once MagSafe wireless charging arrived.

name: iPhone 5c
desc: The plastic, candy-colored iPhone marketed as the affordable option, sold alongside the premium 5s. It was outsold by the 5s in nearly every market and ended Apple's brief experiment with a mid-tier consumer iPhone.

name: iPhone X
desc: Apple's tenth-anniversary iPhone, the first with Face ID and an edge-to-edge OLED display. Discontinued exactly one year after launch, an unusually short lifespan for a flagship iPhone.

name: iPhone 13 mini
desc: The last iPhone with a small screen, beloved by a vocal minority and ignored by everyone else. Apple killed the mini line in 2023, ending a brief experiment in giving people what they said they wanted.

name: Apple Watch (Series 0)
desc: The original Apple Watch, launched in 2015 amid breathless hype as the future of wearables. Sluggish and short on battery, it was retroactively rebranded as 'Series 0' once Series 1 arrived 18 months later.

name: Sherlock
desc: The system search tool in Mac OS that hunted across local files, the web, and online databases. Replaced by Spotlight in 2005 and remembered as the origin of 'sherlocked', the verb for when Apple kills a third-party app by building its features in.

name: iChat
desc: Apple's instant messaging client, supporting AIM, Jabber, and Bonjour. The colorful chat bubbles became iconic before iChat was rebranded as Messages and folded into iMessage in OS X Mountain Lion.

name: Safari for Windows
desc: Apple's attempt to bring Safari to Windows, announced by Steve Jobs as part of his iPhone web strategy. Plagued by security holes and strange font rendering, it was quietly abandoned in 2012 with vulnerabilities still unpatched.

name: Mac OS 9
desc: The final version of the classic Mac OS, foundation of Apple computing for 18 years. Steve Jobs literally held a funeral for it on stage at WWDC 2002, complete with eulogy, organ music, and an open coffin.

name: OpenDoc
desc: Apple's ambitious component-based document framework, designed to replace monolithic apps with modular parts. Steve Jobs killed it within months of returning to Apple in 1997, calling it overengineered and a distraction from shipping a real OS.

name: AppleTalk
desc: The networking protocol that let any Mac on a LocalTalk cable share files and printers with zero configuration. Phased out in macOS Snow Leopard after 24 years, as Apple standardized on TCP/IP and Bonjour.

name: iTunes Festival
desc: Apple's annual month-long free concert series at London's Roundhouse, featuring artists from Adele to Pharrell streamed live to iTunes users. Renamed Apple Music Festival in 2015 and quietly ended after the 2016 season.

name: Photo Stream
desc: Apple's free photo sync service that automatically pushed the last 1,000 photos to all your Apple devices for 30 days. Replaced by iCloud Photos and finally shut down in 2023 after running quietly in the background for 12 years.

name: FireWire
desc: Apple's high-speed serial bus, co-developed with Sony and TI for digital video and audio professionals. Faster than USB at launch, beaten by cheaper USB at every revision, and removed from the last MacBook Pro in 2012.

name: MagSafe (laptop)
desc: The magnetic power connector that detached harmlessly when someone tripped over the cable, saving countless MacBooks from broken ports. Removed in 2016 for USB-C, then quietly restored five years later when Apple admitted USB-C was not actually better.
